metadata.title: A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media

abstract: This study aims to explore social media content associated with cryptocurrency marketing. We employ unsupervised Latent Dirichlet allocation topic modeling and sentiment analysis techniques to 98,716 tweets to examine Twitter (now known as X) content for subjects and sentiments related to cryptocurrency. Our findings reveal that cryptocurrency tweets fell into four categories, with 'cryptocurrency trading, ' 'NFT airdrop, ' 'cryptocurrency affiliate program, ' and 'Dogecoin on social media' being the most popular. Furthermore, most of these topics exhibited positive sentiments. This study contributes theoretically by integrating cryptocurrency marketing into the diffusion of innovation paradigm. In addition, it offers strategic insights for digital marketers in identifying prevalent topics and sentiments related to cryptocurrency, enabling the tailoring of affiliate marketing communication strategies on social media.

sections.paper_id: Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
paragraphs: Given the unprecedented impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the global economy, there has been an increased demand for cryptocurrency investment (Jabotinsky and Sarel 2021; Kim 2021). The global cryptocurrency market cap has surpassed $2 trillion, with the U.S. market expected to exceed $2.2 billion by 2026 (Mediakix 2021). As of 10 March 2024, one Bitcoin was worth $69,115. According to a Pew Research Center survey (Faverio and Sidoti 2023), ~88% of Americans have at least some familiarity with cryptocurrency, and 17% of the respondents indicated that they had invested, traded, or otherwise utilized it.

Given the growing popularity of cryptocurrency investment, previous research has primarily focused on consumer perceptions of cryptocurrency value (Arli et al. 2021), predictors of intention to use cryptocurrencies (Al-Omoush, Gomez-Olmedo, and Funes 2024), and attitudinal responses to Bitcoin usage behavior (Kim 2021), primarily through cross-sectional surveys. Traditional survey approaches often rely on a relatively small and manageable set of self-reported data, which may overlook patterns in cryptocurrency-related conversations. However, few studies have empirically utilized computational text analysis (Walter and Ophir 2019) to identify trends in cryptocurrency discussions on social media. In recent years, social media has emerged as a valuable resource for understanding people's opinions on cryptocurrency investment and their perceptions of the financial risks involved (Poongodi et al. 2021).

To address this gap, we utilize Twitter (now referred to as X) as a data source to explore public perceptions and sentiments surrounding cryptocurrency. This platform has gained significant popularity among marketers and advocates as a communication channel for obtaining cryptocurrency information. Many blockchain companies are eager to promote their cryptocurrency business on Twitter. For instance, former Twitter CEO Jack Dorsey affirmed to investors that Bitcoin would be crucial to the company's future success (Perez 2021). An outspoken proponent of cryptocurrencies, Elon Musk, the CEO of Tesla, Twitter, and SpaceX, has been an avid Twitter user. He finalized the acquisition of Twitter in October 2022 (Conger and Hirsch 2022). Indeed, financial information related to cryptocurrencies frequently trends on Twitter because social media users often share their thoughts and sentiments regarding cryptocurrency investments, news, and affiliate marketing information with others.

Importantly, affiliate marketing operates as a distinctive form of advertising in which advertisers compensate solely for actual sales or conversions generated through the affiliate's endeavors. According to Edelman and Brandi (2015), affiliate marketing seamlessly integrates substantial performance incentives with the broader benefits of online advertising. This strategy aligns with the performance-based nature of social media advertising because marketers can effectively target diverse audience segments, leverage the viral content of social media, and measure the impact of their affiliate marketing campaign efforts (Olbrich, Schultz, and Bormann 2019). For example, when user shares affiliate links or discount codes in their social media posts, they have the opportunity to monetize their following by earning money from subsequent ad sales.

Building on the diffusion of innovation theory (Rogers 2003), the primary objective of this study is to examine how social media shapes the opinions and sentiments of social media users regarding cryptocurrency. Specifically, we aim to identify and evaluate the various topics and perspectives related to cryptocurrency marketing communication efforts. We employ two well-established text mining approaches-topic modeling and sentiment analysis-to extract valuable information from a collection of texts (Dahal, Kumar, and Li 2019;Delgosha, Hajiheydari, and Saadeatmantesh 2020).

Engaging with text-mining approaches can reinforce the diffusion of innovation theory by providing a comprehensive understanding of how new ideas spread through textual data analysis in several ways, such as discovering opinion dynamics, discovering early adopters and influencers, and monitoring adoption patterns. By employing text mining techniques, researchers can systematically analyze vast amounts of textual data, to identify patterns, trends, and insights related to the adoption and spread of innovations. In this study, text mining aids in extracting key concepts and trends associated with innovation diffusion. Analyzing social media content allows researchers to pinpoint influential ideas, terminology, and evolving themes within the discourse surrounding innovations. Through sentiment analysis, text mining helps gauge public sentiment and attitudes toward innovations.

This study makes both theoretical and practical contributions to literature. We conducted a quantitative investigation of cryptocurrency marketing issues gleaned from Twitter and connected them with the diffusion of innovation theory (Rogers 2003) to methodological advancements in computer science. By utilizing text mining techniques, including topic modeling and sentiment analysis, to analyze a Twitter corpus about cryptocurrency, we provide managerial insights that can help digital marketers enhance their strategies for affiliate marketing communication.

sections.paper_id: Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
paragraphs: Cryptocurrencies, such as Bitcoin and Ethereum are digital currencies that use blockchain technology and a decentralized peer-to-peer network to verify financial transactions between users (Narayanan et al. 2016). Cryptocurrency has encrypted, decentralized, and anonymous structures that differ from traditional currencies issued and regulated by governments (Casino, Dasaklis, and Patsakis 2019). Although cryptocurrency is generally considered an alternative form of digital currency, it does not have legal tender status in the United States (Gent 2022). Cryptocurrency highlights cyberlibertarianism by rejecting government authority (Böhme et al. 2015). Given that Bitcoin is restricted to 21 million coins, its market value is driven mainly by the number of coins circulated (i.e. supply) and how much users are willing to pay (i.e. demand) (Reiff 2022). Cryptocurrency prices are incredibly volatile and unpredictable (Cowen 2022). Therefore, trading cryptocurrency involves greater risks than trading conventional financial assets, such as stocks (Betancourt and Chen 2021).

Academic research has identified numerous factors influencing consumers' propensity to adopt cryptocurrencies. Alharbi and Sohaib (2021) highlighted the motivators (i.e. optimism and innovativeness) and inhibitors (i.e. insecurity and discomfort) of cryptocurrency adoption behavior. Furthermore, Arli et al. (2021) found that knowledge of cryptocurrencies, trust in the government, and the speed of transactions could enhance consumers' trust in cryptocurrencies. Recent research has shown that consumers have been more likely to use Bitcoin to reduce physical interaction during the COVID-19 pandemic (Kim 2021). Importantly, social media use has been proven to boost individuals' intentions to embrace Bitcoin through attitudes, subjective norms, and perceived behavioral control (Anser et al. 2020). Prior work has suggested that social media sentiment might be an important factor in determining the value of Bitcoin (Mai et al. 2018). Guégan and Renault (2021) discovered that social media investor sentiment is closely associated with daily Bitcoin returns. They further disclosed that the influence of sentiment on Bitcoin returns is centered around the Bitcoin bubble.

Social media's influence on the cryptocurrency market evokes a range of perspectives that reflect a nuanced understanding of this intricate relationship. While some proponents argue that social media platforms significantly shape market sentiments and impact investment decisions, others highlight the potential drawbacks and risks associated with this influence. Mai et al. (2018) study underscores this positive aspect, emphasizing the role of widespread information dissemination through social media as a key predictor of future cryptocurrency value. Additional articles by Cary (2021) and Huynh (2021) identify the impact of social media communication, including sentiment, on cryptocurrencies. In contrast, the Federal Trade Commission (2021) presents data that emphasizes the market's vulnerability to misinformation and pump-and-dump schemes propagated through social media channels, particularly those associated with celebrities. This perspective cautions against the rapid spread of unverified information, highlighting the potential for volatile market behavior and harm to investors. Considering these diverse viewpoints, the impact of social media on the cryptocurrency market has emerged as a complex interplay between information dissemination, community dynamics, and the imperative for regulatory scrutiny.

paragraphs: Affiliate marketing has become a very important business strategy for all companies that conduct business online to increase customer acquisition (Duffy 2005). According to Dwivedi, Rana, and Alryalat (2017), an affiliate marketing program is a performance-based digital advertising technique in which one actor (e.g. an online merchant) enters into an agreement with another actor (e.g. an affiliate or publisher) to advertise a link from the merchant's website on social media sites. If a user visits an affiliate's website or social media account, clicks the affiliate's link to the merchant, and then makes a purchase from the merchant, the affiliate receives a commission (Edelman and Brandi 2015;Mangió and Di Domenico 2022).

As a new form of marketing in the financial services industry, affiliate marketing seems to be more stable than other forms of online marketing (Stern 2022). Many cryptocurrency companies, such as Binance and Coinbase, use affiliate marketing programs. Affiliate programs provide their members (e.g. social media influencers) with the opportunity to earn referral commissions in exchange for promoting affiliate links to potential audiences. Major cryptocurrencies and fiat currencies are used for payouts, making it possible to earn free coins (e.g. Bitcoin and Ethereum) through affiliate marketing programs. For example, Binance pays its affiliates up to 50% referral trading fees, depending on how many people sign up after clicking on their affiliate link (Mileva 2022). Binance has its own affiliate network, where influencers have at least 5000 followers on Instagram, YouTube, Twitter, Facebook, and so on (Mileva 2022). Furthermore, social media users can participate in affiliate marketing programs, such as mobile games and surveys in exchange for cryptocurrency.

sections.header_raw: Diffusion of information on social media
sections.header_norm: diffusion of information on social media
paragraphs: Diffusion of innovation theory offers a conceptual framework for demonstrating the pace and pattern of acceptance of new products, services, and ideas. Rogers (2003) introduced diffusion as a communication phenomenon that emphasizes the various roles of individual 'adopters' (i.e. innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, and laggards) in altering the transmission of innovations within certain social systems. According to Akaka, Schau, and Vargo (2022), diffusion is 'less of an outcome and more of a co-creative process requiring ongoing engagement from a variety of market-related actors, including consumers, organizations, and policymakers' (p. 941). Social media revolves around diffusing and sharing information through one's social network (John 2013). Thus, examining the information dissemination process on social media falls within the scope of this theory (Ma, Lee, and Goh 2014).

Diffusion of innovation theory has been used extensively to explain information diffusion on Twitter (Chang 2010). Twitter is recognized as one of the major social media platforms for digital marketers using hashtags to advertise their brands and communicate with consumers (Wu et al. 2022). Twitter has frequently been used to predict the popularity of viral topics and the spread of information (Hoang and Mothe 2018). Numerous studies have examined predictions of information diffusion and propagation on Twitter. For example, Hoang and Mothe (2018) used the machine learning model of a random forest classifier and found that tweet diffusion is significantly influenced by user-based (e.g. total number of tweets and number of followers), time-based (e.g. holiday, week, and daytime), and content-based (e.g. media attachment, content size, and sentiment level) features. As Gupta, Sharma, and Thomas (2021) suggested, the reciprocity of the network toward the topic (e.g. blockchain) is critical for effectively determining the information diffusion process on Twitter. In the context of promoted ads, sponsored tweets from Olympians tend to utilize language that is less authentic compared to the athletes' non-sponsored tweets, indicating how Twitter is used to disseminate social media messages with genuine information, which in turn affects users' social media interactions (Fox and Royne Stafford 2021).

As such, Twitter may have a significant influence on financial markets by disseminating information about news and earnings events. There is evidence that Twitter activity boosts daily trading volume (Sprenger et al. 2014). Rakowski, Shirley, and Stark (2021) investigated the impact of Twitter attention on stock prices by analyzing over 21 million company-specific tweets. They found that Twitter attention is closely linked to positive stock returns. Twitter activity may help investors obtain information that is already available through traditional news channels and accelerate the spread of financial information. Furthermore, Inamdar et al. (2019) proposed a prediction model in which tweets were analyzed for sentiment using the Random Forest Regression algorithm and found that sentiment scores have little effect on cryptocurrency pricing. Similarly, recent work (Zaman, Yaqub, and Saleem 2022) has shown that the number of tweets referencing Bitcoin surged after Elon Musk's Twitter profile changed. This surge in tweets has a significant positive association with Bitcoin's price.

Although most studies offer some insights into the factors influencing cryptocurrency/Bitcoin adoption, few have specifically explored public opinion and sentiments about cryptocurrency promotion using a computational approach or the potential for digital promotion on social media. This research aims to bridge this significant gap.

While there have been some general discussions on cryptocurrency (e.g. Bitcoin) in the social media context (Anser et al. 2020;Bolici et al. 2020;Gupta, Sharma, and Thomas 2021;Mai et al. 2018), a thorough computational analysis of social media content related to cryptocurrency promotion is relatively lacking. Moreover, the exploration of sentiments and emotional responses in social media discussions on cryptocurrency has not been the focus of much research. Our study aims to deepen the knowledge of how social media users discuss and feel about cryptocurrency-related content on Twitter. Therefore, we have formulated research questions to steer our preliminary inquiry. Our study aims to probe the topics and sentiments that arise when people discuss cryptocurrency on Twitter. This investigation will ensure that digital marketers are aware of the factors that must be considered as they continue to fine-tune cryptocurrency marketing communication strategies. Together, we propose the following research questions.
sections.text:
Diffusion of innovation theory offers a conceptual framework for demonstrating the pace and pattern of acceptance of new products, services, and ideas. Rogers (2003) introduced diffusion as a communication phenomenon that emphasizes the various roles of individual 'adopters' (i.e. innovators, early adopters, early majority, late majority, and laggards) in altering the transmission of innovations within certain social systems. According to Akaka, Schau, and Vargo (2022), diffusion is 'less of an outcome and more of a co-creative process requiring ongoing engagement from a variety of market-related actors, including consumers, organizations, and policymakers' (p. 941). Social media revolves around diffusing and sharing information through one's social network (John 2013). Thus, examining the information dissemination process on social media falls within the scope of this theory (Ma, Lee, and Goh 2014).

Diffusion of innovation theory has been used extensively to explain information diffusion on Twitter (Chang 2010). Twitter is recognized as one of the major social media platforms for digital marketers using hashtags to advertise their brands and communicate with consumers (Wu et al. 2022). Twitter has frequently been used to predict the popularity of viral topics and the spread of information (Hoang and Mothe 2018). Numerous studies have examined predictions of information diffusion and propagation on Twitter. For example, Hoang and Mothe (2018) used the machine learning model of a random forest classifier and found that tweet diffusion is significantly influenced by user-based (e.g. total number of tweets and number of followers), time-based (e.g. holiday, week, and daytime), and content-based (e.g. media attachment, content size, and sentiment level) features. As Gupta, Sharma, and Thomas (2021) suggested, the reciprocity of the network toward the topic (e.g. blockchain) is critical for effectively determining the information diffusion process on Twitter. In the context of promoted ads, sponsored tweets from Olympians tend to utilize language that is less authentic compared to the athletes' non-sponsored tweets, indicating how Twitter is used to disseminate social media messages with genuine information, which in turn affects users' social media interactions (Fox and Royne Stafford 2021).

sections.paper_id: Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
paragraphs: We collected data using Twitter Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) from 12 June to 11 August 2021. This specific timeframe was selected to capture the recent Twitter conversations related to cryptocurrency. We chose this period because Elon Musk tweeted on 12 May 2021, that Tesla would no longer accept Bitcoin as payment, which might have been potentially significant for the cryptocurrency market. It is worth noting that the sentiment expressed in Elon Musk's tweets aligned with price and volume fluctuations in the Bitcoin market (Zaman, Yaqub, and Saleem 2022). To compile tweets related to cryptocurrency for this study, we gathered tweets that included the following hashtags over the two-month period: #crypto, #cryptocurrency, #bitcoin, #ethereum, and #blockchain.

A hashtag, prefaced with '#' in a tweet, is a word or phrase that is assigned by users for categorizing and searching tweets. Hashtags have often been treated as terms bearing significant value within a topic and thus have been involved in various contexts of topic analysis on Twitter (Alash and Al-Sultany 2020;Cui et al. 2019;Zhu et al. 2017). In this study, we leveraged hashtags to identify prominent trending topics within the Twitter data we collected. The most frequently used hashtags, which appeared in more than 90% of the tweets, as well as infrequently occurring hashtags that appeared fewer than three times were eliminated for further analysis because they tend to carry minimal significance in machine learning. This sampling procedure yielded a total of 98,716 tweets. Among these, ~35.5% (i.e. 35,075) of the collected tweets did not contain any hashtags, while the remaining 64.5% (i.e. 63,641) included at least one hashtag.

sections.paper_id: Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
paragraphs: The latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) (Blei, Ng, and Jordan 2003) is known as the most widely used topic modeling technique for discovering latent thematic structure within a given corpus (Joo, Choi, and Baek 2018;Negara, Triadi, and Andryani 2019). In the context of topic modeling with LDA, several parameters can be adjusted to control the behavior of the algorithm. For the topic modeling with LDA, this study adopted textmindR library in R language and FitLdaModel function. Here are the parameters set with the library and function: Gibbs sampling (default), 5 for the number of topics per documents, alpha = 0.1 to control the sparsity of the document-topic distributions, beta = 0.05 to control the sparsity of the topic-word distributions, 1000 for the number of iterations to control the convergence of the algorithm. In LDA, topics are represented as multinomial distributions over words based on the assumption that a document is characterized by a mixture of topics, and each topic are produced by a distribution of words in documents. To address RQ1, we applied the LDA method using the 'testmindR' package in R programming to the collected dataset of hashtags. Sentiment analysis involves the extraction of emotions or opinions from natural language. While most sentiment analysis studies typically focus on determining whether a tweet is positive or negative, this study applied sentiment analysis to Twitter data to extract emotional information at the word/unigram level, not at the sentence level, from tweets on each topic (identified in RQ1). For this analysis, we employed the 'tidytext' package in R programming language and the NRC Emotion lexicon (Mohammad, Kiritchenko, and Zhu 2013). The selection of the NRC Emotion lexicon was based on two key considerations: (1) its capacity to detect the sentiments of individual terms within messages, aligning with the focus of our study, and (2) its coverage of not only sentiment polarity but also various emotions. The NRC lexicon (http://saifmohammad. com/WebPages/NRC-Emotion-Lexicon.htm) comprises 14,182 English words and their associations with eight fundamental emotions (anger, fear, anticipation, trust, surprise, sadness, joy, and disgust) and two sentiments (negative and positive).

sections.header_raw: RQ1: What topics are discussed in relation to cryptocurrencies on Twitter?
sections.header_norm: rq1 what topics are discussed in relation to cryptocurrencies on twitter
paragraphs: Our study first identified the top four topics concerning cryptocurrency-related hashtags and then ranked the word likelihood to classify each subject. To determine the optimal number of topics, we assessed the LDA coherence scores and found that four had the highest coherence score (0.34) (see Figure 1 for LDA coherence scores).

As shown in Table 2, Topic 1 (cryptocurrency trading) covered buying, selling, and transferring cryptocurrencies. For example, a Twitter post in this category (e.g. 'Buy BTC with your credit card. Fast crypto exchange.') discussed the potential benefits for investors through cryptocurrency transactions, mentioning several virtual currencies including Binance Coin (BNB), Ripple (XRP), and Altcoin. This entails speculating on price fluctuations using cryptocurrency exchanges, such as Binance, which has become the most popular cryptocurrency trading platform.

Topic 2 (NFT airdrop) addressed the distribution of NFTs, with an example tweet expressing enthusiasm (e.g. 'With the opportunity to take part in this NFT airdrop, I am very enthusiastic because this is an excellent project. Let's take it to the moon.'). Generally, a non-fungible token (NFT) is a digital asset representing a one-of-a-kind, immutable cryptographic token on a blockchain. It is usually built and coded using the same programming as cryptocurrencies, such as Bitcoin and Ethereum (Conti and Schmidt 2022). Decentralized finance (Defi), such as NFTs, is a blockchain-based financial system that enables investors to engage in financial activities, such as payments, loans, savings, and trading (Iredale 2021). A gift or incentive given as a sign of appreciation is called an NFT airdrop, which helps marketers achieve their advertising purpose (Perlman 2022).

Topic 3 (cryptocurrency affiliate programs) discussed how to play crypto mobile games and earn cryptocurrency rewards from affiliate marketing programs (e.g. 'I found Ethereum in a Blue vault at this location. Join me playing #coinhuntworld. It's awesome.'). Affiliate marketing is rapidly becoming a significant source of new cryptocurrency users. Crypto affiliate marketing programs, such as Binance and Coinbase, give users up to 50% of the trading fees they earn from referring other people to trade (Kemmerer 2022). An example of content found on Twitter is a mobile game called CoinHuntWorld. The game provides players with the opportunity to play and earn cryptocurrency prizes.

Topic 4 (Dogecoin on social media) covered how Dogecoin became famous on social media (e.g. 'Thanks so much for this gift. I'm just posting an update to say I got back 102K #Doge in my wallet.'). The popularity of Dogecoin has been bolstered by the fervor of social media users and the influence of memes (Avan-Nomayo 2021). Social media users may earn Dogecoin through affiliate marketing on Twitter. On 21 June 2021, Elon Musk stated that Tesla would start accepting Dogecoin as payment for goods. He has been a strong supporter of Dogecoin, tweeting about anything from having SpaceX place a 'literal Dogecoin on the literal moon' to mining the joke token with his children (Ossinger 2022). Overall, tweets about cryptocurrencies demonstrated the link between social media buzz and Dogecoin's price spike (Browne 2021).

Our word cloud analysis identified the most frequently used hashtags, which may help marketers understand how social media users feel about cryptocurrency or what its important features are. As shown in Figure 2, the top 25 hashtags shown in the word cloud with a maximum frequency of 30,000 may help emphasize the most commonly referenced words. Highlighted words like 'bitcoin,' 'crypto,' 'ethereum,' 'btc,' 'blockchain,' 'dogecoin,' and 'affiliate marketing' might reassure social media users that cryptocurrency is a worthwhile investment.

Our word cloud analysis identified the most frequently used hashtags, which may help marketers understand how social media users feel about cryptocurrency or what its important features are. As shown in Figure 2, the top 25 hashtags shown in the word cloud with a maximum frequency of 30,000 may help emphasize the most commonly referenced words. Highlighted words like 'bitcoin,' 'crypto,' 'ethereum,' 'btc,' 'blockchain,' 'dogecoin,' and 'affiliate marketing' might reassure social media users that cryptocurrency is a worthwhile investment.

paragraphs: In the previous section, four topics were identified and described using the hashtags from the harvested Twitter data. The 100 most frequently occurring hashtags are listed for each topic (see Figure 3), and sentiment analysis was applied to the top 100 hashtags.

The sentiment analysis includes eight emotions and two sentiments, and the results are shown in Figure 4. The resultant emotion data show that anticipation, joy, surprise, and trust are the salient positive emotions, whereas negative emotions, such as disgust, fear, and sadness seldom occur in the overall hashtags. At the topic level, the relative intensity of the salient emotions is strong in Topic 4 (Dogecoin on social media), moderate in Topics 2 (NFT airdrop) and 3 (cryptocurrency affiliate program), and weak in Topic 1 (cryptocurrency trading). The sentiment data demonstrates strong positive sentiment across all topics and weak negative sentiment across all topics.

sections.paper_id: Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
paragraphs: The COVID-19 outbreak ushered in unprecedented changes in global stock markets and economies, potentially impacting cryptocurrency markets as alternative investments (Béjaoui et al. 2021). Consequently, it is not surprising that more cryptocurrency marketing information is being posted on social media. Among the myriad of social media platforms, Twitter has emerged as a pivotal channel for the transmission of knowledge and information related to cryptocurrency investment (Ruiz Roque da Silva, Junior, and Balbi 2022). This study conducted textual information analysis using topic modeling and sentiment analysis.

Theoretically, this study extends upon existing literature, which has primarily focused on examining the relationship between Twitter activity and cryptocurrency price change. Previous research has predominantly focused on how cryptocurrency information dissemination on Twitter affects financial performance indicators, such as Bitcoin price fluctuations and stock returns (Rakowski, Shirley, and Stark 2021;Sprenger et al. 2014;Zaman, Yaqub, and Saleem 2022). However, little attention has been paid to how social media users react to Twitter's cryptocurrency marketing practices. Our sentiment analysis revealed that the overall polarity of cryptocurrency-related tweets was positive because the positive emotions of anticipation, joy, surprise, and trust were far more prevalent than the negative emotions of disgust, fear, and sadness. While positive sentiments prevail throughout the various topics investigated in this study, Topic 4, centered around Dogecoin on social media, stands out as a singular subject garnering the highest percentages of positive comments across all positive sentiments. Notably, the four positive emotions-anticipation, joy, surprise, and trustare more prominently expressed in Topic 4 (Dogecoin on Social Media) compared to the other topics under consideration. Since Dogecoin was originally created as a joke cryptocurrency based on the popular Doge meme, its unexpected rise may have elicited emotional reactions of anticipation, joy, and surprise among social media users discussing the volatility of its pricing.

One plausible explanation for Dogecoin's prominence as a trending topic on Twitter is that social media users are likely to discuss and invest in Dogecoin with the expectation of making substantial profits within a short span, primarily because of its price volatility (Scribner 2021). Moreover, Dogecoin's price increased significantly after tweets were made by Elon Musk and Mark Cuban, the owner of the Dallas Mavericks basketball team (Browne 2021). Both Musk and Cuban frequently posted tweets endorsing the Doge meme-inspired cryptocurrency. Consequently, their engagement triggered subsequent discussions on Twitter and heightened interest among users. The interaction between influential figures and the Twitter community played a pivotal role in sustaining Dogecoin's visibility.

Furthermore, our findings contribute to the expanding field of research on cryptocurrency marketing using text mining techniques to identify, collect, and analyze Twitter data about cryptocurrency. Previous literature has predominantly employed survey techniques and investigated audience factors that influence behavioral intentions to use cryptocurrency (Anser et al. 2020;Arli et al. 2021;Kim 2021;Saiedi, Broström, and Ruiz 2021). To the best of our knowledge, our study is the first to undertake a comprehensive computational analysis of Twitter users' sentiments and opinions regarding cryptocurrencies.

Finally, our findings contribute to the extant literature on the impact of social media on cryptocurrency investment. For instance, Zaman, Yaqub, and Saleem (2022) analyzed the impact of Elon Musk's Twitter bio update on Bitcoin prices using sentiment analysis and text mining. They demonstrated that this update not only increased the volume of tweets mentioning Bitcoin but also coincided with a rise in its price. Whereas previous studies (Guégan and Renault 2021;Mai et al. 2018) have explored the influence of social media activities on Bitcoin's value, our study specifically focuses on how social media users discuss and react to cryptocurrency ad campaigns on Twitter.

From a managerial perspective, the surge in cryptocurrency growth and popularity holds significant implications for digital marketers and has the potential to transform the future of the payment business and marketplace. Numerous financial product and service companies, such as Coinbase and PayPal, are looking to promote their cryptocurrency infrastructures in the digital era. For instance, in June 2021, Google Ads introduced a new guideline for cryptocurrency ads: advertisers should request accreditation from Google Ads for new cryptocurrency exchanges and wallets (Helms 2021). Digital marketers could leverage the text mining strategy employed in this study in various ways, including the discovery of insights from dialogues with social media users and the development of more effective cryptocurrency marketing campaigns.

For cryptocurrency affiliate marketers, the text mining methods employed in this study can provide a strategic approach to identifying trends and crafting targeted social media campaigns. For instance, Binance, one of the world's largest cryptocurrency exchanges, harnesses the power of an in-house affiliate network to partner with social media influencers and opinion leaders (Mileva 2022). Text mining can be instrumental for crypto exchange platforms (e.g. Binance) in identifying potential affiliates by analyzing discussions on social media platforms as well as online community groups (e.g. Reddit). This strategy enables marketers to collaborate with social media influencers who are deeply engaged with their followers and possess an extensive understanding of cryptocurrency topics. By analyzing conversations and comments from cryptocurrency users on social media, affiliate marketers can personalize their advertising messaging and promotional offerings to better align with the needs and interests of their target customers.

Policymakers are increasingly concerned about 'pump and dump' schemes, where misleading or false information is spread on social media to manipulate cryptocurrency prices. An example is the involvement of Sam Bankman-Fried, founder of FTX, in a criminal trial that significantly impacted the cryptocurrency industry (Regnier 2023). This situation underscores the need for stricter regulatory frameworks to oversee cryptocurrency advertising and the dissemination of information on these platforms. Such policies might include enhanced monitoring of social media activities related to cryptocurrencies, establishing clear guidelines for ethical advertising, and implementing stringent penalties for misleading promotions.

sections.paper_id: Baek and Yi - 2025 - A computational approach to cryptocurrency marketing on social media
paragraphs: Our study has certain limitations and offers avenues for future research. First, owing to the nature of text mining analysis, we can only compare the patterns of topic distribution and sentiment degree. However, we are unable to establish a causal link between the two. This may be a drawback of computational data analysis (Yun, Pamuksuz, and Duff 2019). Caution should be exercised when interpreting our findings regarding the role of emotion on social media (Kim, Childs, and Baek 2023). Social media users experiencing fear of missing out (FOMO) may anticipate the illusion of positive returns from investing in cryptocurrencies (Anaza et al. 2024). To enhance the robustness of future experiments, it would be worthwhile to integrate a state measure of FOMO. This will allow us to investigate whether FOMO could influence emotional responses to cryptocurrency marketing on social media.

Furthermore, we confined the scope of our investigation by employing unsupervised text mining without the involvement of human coders. Nevertheless, it is plausible to argue that human coding has the potential to analyze user-generated content on social media more accurately in terms of topic categorization compared to relying solely on machine learning-based data mining (Feng, Chen, and He 2019). Future researchers may examine this possibility by offering human coders intense training sessions to generate a refined training set that would improve the topic categorization accuracy of computational technologies (Johnson et al. 2021).

Second, our conclusions were based only on the Twitter dataset. Future research may utilize our proposed textual analytic method to compare cryptocurrency-related content on various social networking sites, such as YouTube, Facebook, and Instagram. In addition, the rapid evolution of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies has spurred significant research interest in the utilization of AI agents for financial investment decision-making (Baek and Kim 2023a;Saggu and Ante 2023). Exploring the potential impact of virtual influencers' recommendations (Haikel-Elsabeh 2023; Saad 2023; Wan and Jiang 2023) on consumers' intentions to purchase cryptocurrencies would be intriguing. Given the ongoing advancement of generative AI technologies in the advertising sector (Baek 2023;Baek and Kim 2023b;Zhang and Rodgers 2023), future research should explore how users evaluate AI-generated cryptocurrency information.

Finally, our data covers a two-month period (from 12 June to 11 August) of the year 2021. Presumably, content type patterns vary over time. The cryptocurrency market may remain turbulent and vary as a result of recent US inflation (Gailey 2022). Future researchers might consider replicating this study for different time periods, such as before and after announcements from the Federal Reserve Board regarding interest rates, to assess the consistency of findings.
